data.source: Online self-administered questionnaire distributed via Facebook and WhatsApp social media groups
data.wave_or_subset: University students in Jordan, May 2020; aged 18+; enrolled in undergraduate or postgraduate programs
data.notes: Non-probabilistic convenience sampling; cross-sectional design; K10 scale (10 items) used to measure psychological distress; responses weighted 1-5 with total score 10-50; categories: no (10-19), mild (20-24), moderate (25-29), severe (30-50); Cronbach’s alpha reported as 0.88 for K10; majority of respondents were undergraduates; potential self-selection bias due to social media recruitment.

method.description: Cross-sectional online survey of Jordanian university students conducted in May 2020 using the Kessler Psychological Distress Scale (K10) alongside sociodemographic and COVID-19-related questions. Data analysis included descriptive statistics, Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test for gender comparisons, Spearman’s rank correlation for associations with age, and multinomial logistic regression to analyze factors associated with psychological distress categories and motivation for distance learning.
steps: Design an online, self-administered questionnaire including the K10 scale and items on demographics, distance learning motivation, coping strategies, medication use, and concerns.
Distribute the survey link via Facebook and WhatsApp groups targeting Jordanian university students aged 18+ enrolled in undergraduate or postgraduate programs.
Exclude incomplete responses (16) and retain 381 complete cases.
Compute K10 total scores for each respondent and classify into distress categories (no, mild, moderate, severe).
Conduct descriptive analyses of participant characteristics, distress scores, coping behaviors, medication use, and concerns.
Use Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test to compare mean K10 scores by gender.
Use Spearman’s rank correlation to assess association between age and K10 distress score.
Fit multinomial logistic regression models to examine factors associated with distress categories and with motivation for distance learning.
Report odds ratios, p-values, and key prevalence estimates (e.g., severe distress, motivation levels, coping behaviors).
method.models: Descriptive statistics; Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test; Spearman’s rank correlation; Multinomial logistic regression
method.outcome_variable: K10 psychological distress (total score and categorical severity: no, mild, moderate, severe); motivation for distance learning (e.g., no/low motivation vs higher)
method.independent_variables: Age; gender; distress severity (as predictor of motivation); motivation for distance learning (as predictor of distress)

results.summary: High prevalence of psychological distress was observed among Jordanian university students during COVID-19, with 69.5% reporting severe distress and an average K10 score of 34.2. There was no significant gender difference in mean K10 scores, but females had higher odds of mild and severe distress compared with males in multinomial models. Age was inversely associated with distress, indicating younger students experienced higher distress. More than half of students reported no motivation for distance learning, which was strongly associated with severe psychological distress. Most common coping strategies included increased social media use, online communication with friends, and watching TV/movies; a minority reported medication use. Online distance learning was the top concern.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Odds of mild distress (Male vs Female)
Odds of severe distress (Male vs Female)
Association of severe distress with no motivation for distance learning
Coping by increased social media use
Coping by talking to friends online

numerical_results.effect_size: CNS medications; sedative-hypnotics most common (38% of medication users)
Top concern vs societal isolation (19.7%) and contracting COVID-19 (13.9%)
